votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the factorial function and the Brazilian factorial logic, matching the Coq specification exactly.
The Python code accurately implements the factorial function and the Brazilian factorial logic, matching the Coq specification.
The Python code correctly implements the factorial function and the Brazilian factorial logic by multiplying the factorials of numbers from 1 to n, matching the Coq specification.

The Python fact function matches the Coq fact function. The brazilian_factorial_impl computes the product of fact(1) * fact(2) * ... * fact(n), which matches Coq's fold_right mult 1 (map fact (seq 1 n)) since seq 1 n produces [1, 2, ..., n]. The pre and spec functions also match.

The Python fact function matches the Coq fact function. The brazilian_factorial_impl in Python computes the product of fact(1) * fact(2) * ... * fact(n), which matches Coq's fold_right mult 1 (map fact (seq 1 n)) since seq 1 n produces [1, 2, ..., n]. The pre and spec functions also match.

The Python fact function matches the Coq fact function. The brazilian_factorial_impl computes the product of fact(1) * fact(2) * ... * fact(n), which matches Coq's fold_right mult 1 (map fact (seq 1 n)) since seq 1 n produces [1, 2, ..., n]. The pre and spec functions also match.
Python computes product of factorials for i=1..n exactly like Coq’s fold_right mult 1 (map fact (seq 1 n)); fact and pre/spec mirror Coq. Matches for all natural n (including n=0).
Both Coq and Python compute the product of factorials from 1 to n (using seq 1 n vs range(1, n+1)), with the same factorial definition and precondition n > 0; thus results match on all valid inputs.

Both implementations compute the product of factorials from 1 to n (seq 1 n -> [1..n] vs range(1, n+1)), with matching factorial definitions and the same precondition n > 0. The spec in Python checks equality to the same implementation, so results align for all valid inputs.
